title: No-bake lemon cheesecake
subtitle: Fridge-set cheesecake with a sharp lemon filling
excerpt: A fridge-set cheesecake with a digestive biscuit base and a cream cheese filling sharpened with fresh lemon. No gelatine, no oven. Holds its shape when sliced and cut.
sourceNotes: The no-bake cheesecake is a British domestic formula of the mid-twentieth century. The method (whipped double cream folded into cream cheese for stability without gelatine) is documented in British domestic cookery from the 1960s onwards. The lemon version is one of the two most common flavours alongside strawberry.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the night before and refrigerate overnight. Unmould up to 1 hour before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which whipped cream holds its shape when the beaters are lifted, but the tips curl over rather than standing upright. At soft peaks the cream still flows slightly. Fold it into cream cheese at this stage — over-whipped, stiff cream folds in grainily and gives the cheesecake an uneven texture.

content.text: No-bake cheesecake works on one principle: whipped double cream folded into cream cheese gives the filling enough structure to hold its shape in the fridge, without gelatine. The lemon juice and zest must come from fresh lemons — bottled juice lacks the volatile oils that give fresh lemon its brightness.
Do not over-whip the cream. It should reach
and no further. Stiff cream makes the finished cheesecake grainy.

Crush the {{digestive-biscuits}} to fine crumbs in a food processor or in a sealed bag with a rolling pin. Mix with the melted {{unsalted-butter}} until the crumbs are evenly dampened. Press into the base of the springform tin in an even layer. Refrigerate while you make the filling.

Beat the {{full-fat-cream-cheese}} and {{icing-sugar}} together until smooth. Stir in the zest and juice of the {{lemons}}. In a separate bowl, whip the {{double-cream}} to soft peaks. Fold the whipped cream into the cream cheese in three additions.

Spoon the filling onto the biscuit base and smooth the top. Refrigerate for at least 6 hours, overnight if possible. To unmould, run a warm knife around the inside of the tin before unclipping. Serve immediately.
